---
title: "Pulse Prune"
subtitle: "Click the dim weights. Keep the bright ones."
description: "A browser mini-game from MLSys Playground. Race a 45-second timer to prune 60% of a neural network's weights without crashing accuracy. Teaches magnitude-based pruning."
page-layout: article
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="/assets/games/common.css">
          <script src="/assets/games/common.js" defer></script>
          <script src="/assets/games/prune.js" defer></script>
---

```{=html}
<div class="mlsp-game-container" role="region" aria-label="Pulse Prune mini-game">
  <canvas id="mlsp-canvas" class="mlsp-game-canvas" width="680" height="460"></canvas>
  <div class="mlsp-game-hud">
    <span class="mlsp-score"><span id="mlsp-acc">100.0</span>% acc · <span id="mlsp-spa">0</span>% sparse · <span id="mlsp-time">45</span>s</span>
    <span>click dim weights · <kbd>R</kbd> retry</span>
  </div>
</div>

<div id="mlsp-aha-slot"></div>

<script>
(function mountPulsePrune(){
  function boot() {
    if (!window.MLSP || !MLSP.games || !MLSP.games.prune) return setTimeout(boot, 30);
    var canvas = document.getElementById("mlsp-canvas");
    var $acc   = document.getElementById("mlsp-acc");
    var $spa   = document.getElementById("mlsp-spa");
    var $time  = document.getElementById("mlsp-time");
    var ahaSlot = document.getElementById("mlsp-aha-slot");
    var api = MLSP.games.prune(canvas, {
      onScoreChange: function(s) {
        $acc.textContent = s.accuracy.toFixed(1);
        $spa.textContent = s.sparsity.toFixed(0);
        $time.textContent = Math.ceil(s.timeLeft / 1000);
      },
      onGameOver: function(result) {
        MLSP.showAhaCard(ahaSlot, api.ahaLabel, api.ahaText);
        var card = ahaSlot.querySelector(".mlsp-aha-card");
        if (card && api.buildShareText) {
          var share = document.createElement("button");
          share.type = "button";
          share.textContent = "📋 Copy share text";
          share.style.cssText = "margin-top:0.75rem;background:#a31f34;color:#fff;border:none;padding:0.4rem 0.85rem;border-radius:4px;cursor:pointer;font-size:0.85rem;font-weight:600;";
          share.addEventListener("click", function() {
            var txt = api.buildShareText(result);
            if (navigator.clipboard) {
              navigator.clipboard.writeText(txt).then(function(){
                share.textContent = "✓ Copied!";
                setTimeout(function(){ share.textContent = "📋 Copy share text"; }, 1800);
              });
            }
          });
          card.appendChild(share);
        }
      },
      onRetry: function() { window.location.reload(); }
    });
  }
  boot();
})();
</script>
```

## How it works

A small neural network is drawn on the canvas: a few input neurons, some hidden neurons, a few output neurons, with every pair connected by a weight. **Bright lines are high-magnitude weights. Faint lines are low-magnitude weights.** Occasionally a blue pulse flows from an input through the live connections out to an output — that's an inference running.

Your job: **click the dim weights** to remove them. Every dim cut scores +1. But if you click a bright weight, accuracy drops — because bright weights are doing real work. Reach **60% sparsity** within **45 seconds** while keeping accuracy above **50%** to win.

## Why this matters

Trained neural networks are full of small, near-zero weights that contribute almost nothing to the output. Production compression pipelines cut exactly those — magnitude pruning — and can shrink models 5–20× with minimal accuracy loss. You're doing that same decision by eye: telling the redundant weights apart from the load-bearing ones. The [lottery-ticket hypothesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.03635) suggests the bright survivors are all you ever needed.

---

*Part of [MLSys Playground](/games/).*
